coredata.dc:title: Enhanced charge transport characteristics in zinc oxide nanofibers via Mg2+ doping for electron transport layer in perovskite solar cells and antibacterial textiles

coredata.dc:description: ZnO is well-known electron transport material; however, its charge carrier mobility is restricted due to lower conductivity and hysteresis losses. To overcome these issues, 1–3 wt% Mg-doped ZnO nanofibers were synthesized via electrospinning and then applied as electron transport layer (ETL) of perovskite solar cell. The structural, morphological, chemical composition, electronic structure, and optical activity of the synthesized nanofibers were studied to elucidate the role of Mg doping. X-ray diffraction of all nanofibers revealed that Mg was successfully incorporated into ZnO lattice and revealed that ZnO is present in hexagonal wurtzite structure. Optical characterization of the nanofibers revealed that with an increase in Mg2+ doping concentration, the bandgap energy (Eg) tuned from 3.36 eV to 2.8 eV. It was observed that doping ZnO matrix with Mg ions improved the electronic structure, hence favoring the increase in the fill factor, current density, and efficiency. By doping 1, 2, and 3 wt% Mg into ZnO, efficiency was increased up to 8.48, 10.33, and 13.52%, respectively. Thus, a significant improvement in the performance of ZnO was noted by our proposed facile Mg doping. In addition, a significant improvement in photocatalytic activity was observed in Mg doped ZnO, which was used for fabrication of antibacterial textiles.

SEM images of (a) Undoped ZnO nanofibers, (b) Undoped ZnO nanofibers magnified, (c) Mg-ZnO nanofibers, (d) Mg-ZnO nanofibers magnified, (e) Mg-ZnO nanofiber based perovskite solar cell. (f) Mg-ZnO nano-fibers with 5% Mg-doping.
Absorption spectra of a)-ZnO b)- 1% Mg-ZnO c)- 2% Mg-ZnO b)- 3% Mg-ZnO (e) Band gap spectra of ZnO, 1% Mg-ZnO, 2% Mg-ZnO, 3% Mg-ZnO.

Efficiency parameters (Voc, Jsc, FF, η) of all the solar cell devices.
Comparison of different doped nanofibers ETL based PSCs’ performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnO is well-known electron transport material; however, its charge carrier mobility is restricted due to lower conductivity and hysteresis losses. To overcome these issues, 1–3 wt% Mg-doped ZnO nanofibers were synthesized via electrospinning and then applied as electron transport layer (ETL) of perovskite solar cell. The structural, morphological, chemical composition, electronic structure, and optical activity of the synthesized nanofibers were studied to elucidate the role of Mg doping. X-ray diffraction of all nanofibers revealed that Mg was successfully incorporated into ZnO lattice and revealed that ZnO is present in hexagonal wurtzite structure. Optical characterization of the nanofibers revealed that with an increase in Mg2+ doping concentration, the bandgap energy (Eg) tuned from 3.36 eV to 2.8 eV. It was observed that doping ZnO matrix with Mg ions improved the electronic structure, hence favoring the increase in the fill factor, current density, and efficiency. By doping 1, 2, and 3 wt% Mg into ZnO, efficiency was increased up to 8.48, 10.33, and 13.52%, respectively. Thus, a significant improvement in the performance of ZnO was noted by our proposed facile Mg doping. In addition, a significant improvement in photocatalytic activity was observed in Mg doped ZnO, which was used for fabrication of antibacterial textiles.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic photovoltaic devices have attracted huge attention for renewable energy devices due to their low cost of production, and improved efficiency. The significant technological advances of PSCs have shown their renewable energy potential. Perovskite solar cells PSCs have drawn tremendous attention because of its high power conversion efficiency, suitable band gap, large absorption coefficient, long carrier diffusion length as well as highest current density. Its efficiency is comparable to single-crystalline Si-solar cells. There are numerous obstacles toward PSCs commercialization, where stability is one of main concern towards its commercialization. Lower stability is because of high density trap states, existence of dangling bonds on perovskite and degradation of perovskite under ambient conditions. To eliminate these detrimental issues, novel molecules and synthesis strategies are being developed to overcome these concerns [1].

Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are mainly composed of three layers; the electron transport layer (ETL), hole transport layer (HTL), and the absorber layer. Usually, perovskite solar cells employ n-i-p or p-i-n structures where the perovskite layer (intrinsic semiconductor layer) is sandwiched between the n-type ETL and p-type (HTL) [1]. ETL is mainly responsible for electrons transportation, to restrict holes and electrons recombinations. Interestingly, the (ETL) –consisting of metal oxides Zinc oxide (ZnO) or Titania (TiO2)– is a critical determinant of the efficiency of PSCs. Commonly, TiO2, ZnO, and Phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM), are integrated with the HTL of p-type spiro-OMeTAD, NiO, CoN [2]. the material selection in the PSC structure is determined by the energy level alignment, which is favorable for charge separation [3]. After excitation, the photoelectrons should be instantly injected into the conduction band of ETL, while the holes are transported through HTL. In this way, the efficient separation of charge carriers, with suppressed recombinations is responsible for the high photovoltaic performance [4]. To achieve suitable efficiency, an ideal ETL should exhibit high exciting dissociation, suitable absorption capacity, and trapping state densities [5,6]. With this motivation, we tested the potential of Mg doping, in enhancing the optoelectronic properties of ZnO-nano-fibers based ETL.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a common binary semiconductor (II-IV) with high) electron mobility (120 cm2 V-1 s-1). In addition, it is also studied for the diverse application areas for its intrinsic piezoelectricity [7], special optical properties [8,9], photocatalysis, and biochemical interactions [10]. Currently ETL oxides such as ZnO is most demanding due to their suitable band gap (3.37eV at room temperature) and high binding energy (60 meV). In general, ZnO has three distinct crystalline structures: cubic rock salt, cubic zinc blend, and hexagonal wurtzite structure of Zinc oxide [11].

In the recent past, several researchers modified the bandgap and associated charge transport properties of ZnO modified via various dopants. Te Hua Fang et al. [12] demonstrated that with the addition of Mg to ZnO, which tuned the optoelectronic properties with a narrow bandgap [12]. Hence, visible light absorption and better stability in alkaline media were observed by metal doping. Mg improves the structural properties of zinc oxide for photocatalytic and photoanode of dye-sensitized solar cell applications [13]. Extending the idea, this works tests the potential of Mg-doped nano-fibers for the ETL of PSCs. The synthesis process and deposition variety also influences the performance of ETL. Sn doped TiO2 ETL was fabricated by chemical bath deposition, via low temperature solution method directly enhances the stability of PSCs. Sn doped TiO2 low temperature chemical bath deposited is efficient for photo generated electrons extraction and transportation, leading to high stability, current density and efficiency of PSCs. This is an effective route for flexible perovskite solar cell [14]. Low temperature processed tantalum/niobium co-doped titania was reported by Yanyan Duan et al. [15]. Facile low temperature chemical bath deposition route was adopted for co doping, thus achieving efficiency up to 17.66 with pristine TiO2 and 19.44% with doped TiO2. Metal-doped rutile TiO2 thin films having dopant (W, Ta, Nb, or Sn) were prepared by low temperature solution based method. With this dopant, optoelectronic properties of titania were tuned and used for high performance PSCs. This low temperature synthesis route enhances the electron transportation, carrier mobility and power conversion efficiency of ETM upto 16.12% [16]. An efficient low-temperature, Zn doped titania compact layers were synthesized by solution processing and used as ETL for efficient perovskite solar devices. This doped thin layers reduced the Fermi levels and series resistance by Zn doping. High electrons conductivity and efficiency upto 27% was achieved with these PSCs [17].

Bin Wang et al. motivated by adhesion method of mussels hence utilized three catechol derivatives having Lewis base groups as a designed functional groups naming 3,4-Dihydroxyphenethylamine (DA), 3,4-Dihydroxyphenylalanine (DOPA) and 3-(3,4-Dihydroxyphenyl) propionic acid (DPPA). These are behaved as a inter-bridges and passivated between perovskite and SnO2 to reduce the hysteresis losses and to enhance efficiency. These inter-bridges result into secondary bonds formation with Pb2+ and Sn4+ thus PSCs will exhibit higher stability and performance. Upon the DOPA passivation, there is drastic power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 21.5% achieved by planar PSCs. This PSCs show the constant photovoltaic performance even after 55 days of room-temperature storage. This strategy is very much demanding for passivating the buried interfaces of perovskite photovoltaics [18]. Deposition of polycrystalline SnO2 Nano pillars as an electron transport material was studied which lead to enhance the light harvesting and electrons conduction in perovskite solar cells. It showed the efficiency upto 14.9% [19].

1-D materials are promising candidates for ETL layer of high-performance photovoltaic devices, as they offer long transport channels and the least hysteresis losses. Electrospinning has emerged as a promising method for the preparation of 1-D metal-oxide (ZnO and TiO2), due to its rapid, facile, and inexpensive nature, which holds great potential for practical applications [20]. Recently, ZnO nanofibers are doped with different metallic elements such as aluminum (Al) [21], indium (In) [22], and gallium (Ga) to enhance the optical/electrical characteristics [23,24]. Previously, electrospun ZnO lattice was doped with various other metals; however, the potential of Mg-doping is not studied before [25,26]. Defects, interfacial resistance, and morphology are the major attributors to the conversion efficiency of PSCs. In this study pristine ZnO nanofibers synthesized by electrospinning method have shown the low conversion efficiency in comparison to Mg doped ZnO possibly due to the reduce defects and increased electronic conduction with doping. Here, we probed the role of Mg doping, which resulted in significant improvement in the semiconductor properties of ZnO. Also, Mg doped ZnO nanofibers is not reported as ETM in PSCs.

Additionally we also tested the potential of proposed modification in photoactive textile. As photoactive textiles are getting interest after COVID-19 as virus transmission can be suppressed by using photoactive textiles. Antimicrobial agents include: agents that rupture the cell wall synthesis, inhibit protein synthesis, depolarize the cell membrane, inhibit nuclei acid synthesis, and inhibit metabolic pathways in bacteria. Generally metal oxides show antibacterial activity by the mechanism of photocatalysis. In photocatalysis, the exciton produced by the photon absorption generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) [27]. These ROS are of nascent and highly reactive nature, hence can degrade the cell wall of microbes, hence inhibiting their growth and propagation [28,29].

Mg being an earth-abundant and relatively low-cost metal, we optimized its doping concentration in ZnO, with a motivation to improve the charge transport properties of ZnO nanofibers. The ZnO sol-gel with varying concentrations of Mg-doping was electrospun using a single needle, equipped with a controlled feeding system. Three different concentrations i.e. 1, 2, and 3 wt % of Mg doping were tested to enhance the electron transport in ZnO. The effects of Mg2+ ion concentration within the ZnO lattice were analyzed for structural, morphological, Optical, and charge transport behavior. Hall Effect and Current density (I-V characterizations) were used to characterize the electrical and current density parameters of Mg-doped ZnO nano-fibers based ETL in perovskite solar cells. The devices fabricated with optimal doping of Mg in ZnO nanofibers (ETL thickness 440 nm) showed significant enactment improvement (with PCE of 15.03%), paralleled to undoped ZnO nano-fibers (PCE of 6.09%). In addition, we also tested the functionalization of textiles with Mg-doped ZnO structure, which showed significantly better photoactivity, as compared to undoped-ZnO.

For the production of both undoped-ZnO and Mg-doped ZnO nanofibers, the electrospinning route was adopted. Under continuous magnetic stirring, a homogeneous ZnO precursor solution was yielded by dissolving a 1:0.8 mass ratio of zinc nitrate-hexahydrate (Zn (NO3)26H2O) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) into a mixed solvent of ethanol and deionized water (DI) (8:3 V:V). PVP was used as a binder in fibers synthesis. Then this transparent sol was allowed to pass into the nozzle (HT150, HT Solution scientific Inc., U.S.A.) equipped with a syringe pump. The applied potential was kept constant at 15.6 kV and the feeding rate was kept constant at 1.0 mL/min. To prepare the fibers of various thicknesses, the electrospinning time was controlled. Magnesium doping was achieved by adding magnesium chloride (MgCl2) to the aforesaid precursor solution with a doping concentration of 1, 2, 3 wt%. For the collection of nanofibers, fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) was fixed after a deep cleaning with ethanol (5 times) and then with DI water and acetone solution for 15 min. Then these nanofibers were allowed to calcine at 430 °C for 1 h.

The perovskite devices were made using a sequential deposition approach, as reported previously [30]. Ethanol, acetone, and water-washed Florine-doped tin-oxide (FTO) glass having the sintered ZnO fibers used as a substrate for device fabrication. Firstly 1 Molar PbI solution was dissolved into N,N-dimethylformamide. It was then spin-coated on the surface of nanofiber ETL at 6000 rpm for 5 s. The substrate was placed into the oven for drying at 70 °C for half-hour. Then ETL surface was dipped into a 10 mg solution of methyl-ammonium iodide (MAI) prepared in 2-propanol for 20 min. This substrate was then rinsed into E-solution and again dried at 70 °C for 30 min. This substrate was now ready for the deposition of the hole transport layer (HTL). It was comprised sulfonamide lithium salt (LiTFSI, 99.95%) and 9,99-spirobifluorene (Spiro-OMeTAD), 17.5 μL with 28.8 μL of 4-tert-butylpyridine (TBP, 96%) in 1 mL of chlorobenzene (99.9%). It was again spin-coated (at 2500 rpm for 30 s) over the perovskite absorber layer. Finally, Ag electrodes were finally deposited by thermal evaporation. Fig. 1 explains the synthesis of ZnO, 1, 2, 3 wt% electrospun nanofibers and fabrication of perovskite solar cells. In addition we tested the potential of Mg doped fibers for antibacterial properties of textiles.

The undoped-ZnO and Mg-doped ZnO Nanofibers were characterized for structural, morphological, compositional, optical, and electrical properties. X-Ray Diffraction for crystal structure analysis was characterized at D8 Bruker Advanced X-ray Solutions GmbH (Karlsruhe, Germany) instrument equipped with Bragg-Brentano configuration. An X-ray source with a wavelength of 1.5418 Å was used to examine crystal phases. X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out between 10⁰ to 80⁰, with a step size of 0.05⁰. The test readings were then analyzed using JADE software. Nanofibres composition and structural analysis were supported by Raman spectroscopy analysis performed by model Spectra RENISHAW Invia equipped with an excitation laser of 514 nm.

Morphology of the fibers was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Model: JSM-6490A, manufacturer: JEOL, Japan). Optical absorption and bandgap spectra were studied using U.V vis spectroscopy, in the range of (400 nm–800 nm) by using UH4150AD UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer. To analyze the functional groups, Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) analysis was performed using Cary 630 FTIR (Agilent Technologies). The FTIR was performed in the range of 4000–650 cm-1, with a resolution of 2 cm-1. For thermal stability analysis, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on TGA 5500 (TA Instruments) in a Nitrogen environment with an airflow rate of 35 mL/min. Each powder sample (10 mg) was heated up to 900 ᵒC for around 42 min. Hall effect measurements were taken at Swin system of 5300 G magnetic field and readings were taken under dark conditions and at a temperature of 300 K. For solar cell characteristics and to calculate efficiency for all the devices, measurements were taken solar simulator (Newport 94043A) with simulated light (100 m Wcm-2) to calculate current density, fill factor and efficiency. Before the measurement. the equipment was calibrated using the reference silicon solar standard. Qualitative antibacterial testing was measured by Escherichia coli (E.coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) having test standard AATCC 147. Single colony of S. aureus bacteria were carried out into broth. It was initially sterilized at 121 °C for 15 min and further incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. For culture growth it was placed under orbital shaker (150 rpm). It was placed into Petri dish for bacterial inoculum after serial dilution. The samples were placed under UV light for half hour and then into were bacterial lawn for 24 h at 37 °C.

The effect of interfacial modification by Mg2+ on the phase composition and crystallinity of ZnO Nanofibers were studied through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. It is very much essential to understand the ETL influence on the final parameters of solar cells. XRD patterns of the undoped and Mg-doped ZnO nanofibers are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The strongest peaks obtained at 31.1°, 33.7°, and 35.5° can be related to (100), (002), and (101) planes of ZnO, respectively e (JCPDS No. 36- 1451) [31]. The sharp diffraction peaks associated with the ZnO-based hexagonal wurtzite structure pattern can be observed. The absence of additional peaks after Mg doping is because Mg2+ ion has been completely substituted to the Zn2+ ion or as a site of exchange between the ZnO crystal. Instead of making a new crystal (MgO), magnesium is fully doped into ZnO lattice. This is so because of the similarity in ionic radii which supports the complete replacement of Zn with Mg. Instead, there was a slight difference in the intensities of peaks, which means that the Mg doping favors the growth of planes. The peaks in 3% Mg are broadened due to strain effects [32].

Also, it is reported by the previous researcher that Alkaline earth metals have a positive effect on zinc oxide lattice as grain size increases after doping [33]. Due to Mg ions doping within the crystal lattice of ZnO, strain is generated due to which there comes swap of lattice and also change the consistency of lattice as shown in Fig. 2(b). This strain affects crystallinity and also lattice properties are changed due to this. However, a careful decrease of intensities of some peaks shows that the higher position changes towards lower angle values as detected by the higher doping of Mg into the ZnO matrix. In particular, the peak at 30.6° was broadened with increasing dopant concentration, which might be due to the replacement of the Zn2+ ion by the Mg2+ ion. Further crystallite size, microstrain and dislocation density were calculated by using the Sherrer's equation and Williamson and Smallman's relation as presented in following Equations (1)–(3) [34].

Strain analysis and crystallite size was calculated by using Equations (1) and (2), microstrain calculated by equation (3) and presented in Table 1 . Thus crystallite size reduces with the addition of effective dopant concentration. It is because dopant induces resultant drag forces against growth of crystallite size. It leads towards smaller crystallite size. As in case of ETMs, crystallite size reduces from 25 nm to 19 nm after Mg doping. This table also presents decrease in the micro-strain values with the increase in Mg weight percentage which is due to the difference of Mg2+ ionic radius to that of Zn2+ ionic radius when incorporated to the ZnO matrix causing the strain to decrease.

In order to determine the vibrational modes and to support the crystallinity of nanofibers, Raman scattering was studied. Raman results of the Mg-doped ZnO nanofibres compared to pure ZnO, are shown in Fig. 2(c). These vibrational modes support the photons absorption and then electrons transportations at symmetric or asymmetric paths. These paths favor the electron's transport by reducing their recombination rates. The Raman spectra represent that change in peak positions with the addition of Mg dopant as compared to undoped ZnO. The peaks around less than 500 cm-1 mainly due to vibration in ZnO sub-lattice were found at approximately 382, 399, 438, and 474 cm-1sites in all Mg-doped ZnO samples. The peaks at 645 cm-1 show maximum intensity in undoped ZnO at room temperatures [35]. The Raman spectra in the given wavenumber were also reported by Cusco et al. [36]. The peak at 382 cm-1 in Mg-doped Zn of 1, 2, and 3 wt %.is also present and indicates the anomalous mode. This is only due to the doping of Mg into ZnO lattice and improvement in crystallinity. In an undoped ZnO, the maximum value of A1 is 382 cm-1 which can be assigned to low B1 high mode and is found to be switched to another anomalous mode above 394 cm-1. After Mg dopant element may possess vibration complexes due to shifting towards the lower lattice. This shifting is mainly due to stresses generated after Mg dopant into ZnO lattice that may be tensile or shear, caused in certain cases. In 1 and 2 wt % Mg-ZnO, the frequency of T2H mode is high. The high A1 symmetrical peak value 382 cm-1 in the undoped ZnO also has symmetric covenant with the low E2 low-frequency E2. The presence of Mg has flagged it into a broader shoulder. E1-LO mode was not observed for undoped ZnO that were not placed at 590 cm-1, only significantly present in 3 wt Mg- ZnO% as compared to 1 and 2 wt %. This is because in 3 wt % Mg dopant cause more strain generated between ZnO and crystallinity is affected which results in the frequency of vibration of subunits of ZnO-related sequences. Similar peaks are reported in the literature and correspond to different phonon modes at different frequency vibrations [37].

FTIR analysis was performed to characterize the functional groups, chemical bonding, and vibrational of molecules in the lattice. These functional groups, especially hydroxyl groups directly enhanced the photovoltaics parameters. The functional surface provides a better interface for the charge transfer reactions. All the undoped and 1, 2, 3 wt % Mg-doped ZnO Nanofibers FTIR as shown in Fig. 2(d) between wavelength 4000 and 500 cm-1. The FTIR results correspond with the chemical analysis and indicate the successful incorporation of Mg into the ZnO lattice at different concentrations. For Zn-O, vibrational modes are found in the range of 650 cm-1. On the other hand, the widest absorption band located at 3450 cm−1 is attributed to the elongation vibration of O–H bond which reveals the existence of dangling –OH bonds on the surface of ZnO. These vibrational peaks of -OH functional groups are commonly present on the metal oxide nanofibers [24]. By increasing dopant concentration, the –OH groups concentration becomes higher. This can be related to the formation of dangling bonds on the defect-rich surface. In this range, peaks are shifted systematically with the dopant concentration of Mg indicating that it has a strong influence on ZnO lattice and changes its vibrational modes slightly. Vibration between Mg-O was observed around 1400 cm-1 to 1450 cm-1 [38]. The very low intensity of these absorption bands confirms the slight doping concentration Mg, during the synthesized ZnO nanostructures. This can be linked to ionic radiation and systematic changes induced as a result of the collision. In the present case, there is a decrease in the intensity of the vibrational bands because of lattice distortion and the variation in the atomic mass [39].

A scanning electron microscope was used to study the morphological structure. Morphological analysis is critical for enhanced electrons transportation. Nanofibers surface supports better conduction and transport of photogenerated electrons. Fig. 3 (a-d) of undoped ZnO and Mg-doped ZnO fibers were obtained at different resolutions. The inclusion of Mg2+ to ZnO lattice renders an enhanced porosity in the fiber structure but Mg2+ does not change the morphology of ZnO nanofibers. When we doped the Mg2+ in Zinc oxide, some amorphous particles are observed due to the presence of Magnesium. The porous fibrous structure (unlike nano-particles or electrospraying) formed as a result of electrospinning can be clearly observed at high resolution in Fig. 3(b). These nanofibers exhibit the diameter in nano regime supporting large surface areas for electrons conduction, enhanced porosity, and a conjuncted path from absorber layer to electron transportation layer [37]. Fig. 3(d), shows the magnified electrospun fibers Mg-doped ZnO nanofibers scans. All the fibers are elongated, dense, and are able to support photoelectrons from the valence band towards the conduction band for electron channeling to the external circuit. Compared to nanoparticles, the confinement effect in 1D-nanostructures is less, hence better charge transport characteristics are observed in nano-fibers In Fig. 3(e), the side-view of the complete PSC device is shown, It is clearly highlighting glass/FTO/ZnO nanofibers based ETL/MAPbI3 (absorber layer)/spiro-OMeTAD/Ag, respectively. It can be observed from Fig. 3(e) that ETL nanofibers have proper infiltration with perovskite [40]. After increasing dopant concentration upto 5%, fine fibers are not formed, instead beads and fused fiber structure is formed, as shown in Fig. 3(f).

In order to investigate the optoelectronic properties of different concentrations of Mg in ZnO, the UV-VIS characterization was carried out in the solad stated spincoated onto glass substrate. The bandgap was calculated from the absorption value using the Tauc plot equation (1). The absorption spectra of doped and undoped ZnO samples are shown in Fig. 4 (a–d). The absorption of the undoped-ZnO sample is limited to 394 nm; however, Mg doping caused an extended visible-light absorption to a lower wavelength of 413 nm. There is a shift in absorption spectra due to the addition of Mg, because of an increase in the carrier concentration. Mg doping produced intermediate states favorable for electrons excitation at lower energies. It also produces lower energy states, thus narrowing down the bandgap and stabilizing the conduction band known as the Burstein–Moss effect [41].

The bandgap of ZnO fibers was calculated from the UV–Visible results, using the Tauc plot, as shown in Fig. 4 (e). The bandgap (X-intercept of slope in Tauc plot) was obtained by converting the wavelength into energy using the following equation:
(4)
( ∝ h v )
1 / n
= C
(
h v − E g
)
In this equation, “E" is the bandgap energy, “h" is the Planks constant, “c" is the wavelength of light, and "ƛ" is the wavelength of that nanofibers samples.

The bandgap of ZnO was successfully tuned by incorporating Mg2+ into the ZnO lattice. The bandgap for undoped ZnO is 3.37 eV Fig. 4 (e), however, Mg doping supported the decrease in bandgap as the alkaline metal is incorporated completely into the parent atom lattice [21]. With the addition of Mg-dopant, the bandgap is slightly shifted to 3.1, 2.9, and 2.8 eV, with an absorption wavelength at 380 nm known as the absorption edge associated with the bandgap energy being significantly reduced. Samples containing 3% of Mg can be considered an optimal doping concentration as it provides maximum absorption and shifting of the bandgap. Compared to the bandgap of pure-ZnO (3.3 eV), the bandgap of 1% Mg-doped ZnO to 3.1 eV. The band gap difference is shown as following equation
(Equation 5) ΔEg = Eg(A) - Eg(B)

This band gap difference is distributed between the two discontinuities; ΔEv and ΔEc. In alignments, it is generally the case that the conduction band which has the higher energy minimum will bend downward, whilst the valence band which has the lower energy maximum will bend upward to make a mutual junction. Mg doping creates Fermi states between conduction band and valence bands supporting conduction of electrons from valence bands towards conduction band. When intermediate states are formed, band gap is reduced. These results have strong consistency with the literature as Mg is well-known metal to be used as a dopant for photocatalytic activity [42]. Mg dopant acts to reduce the recombination rate and fasten the response time. Surface defects and vacancies generated by doped-Mg play an important role in electron-hole pair generation. Due to the incorporation of Mg dopant into ZnO lattice, the bandgap is systematically reduced due to the formation of intermediate states between conduction and valence band. It was also due to the reduced quantum confinement effect, which has a greater influence on the photovoltaic performance. With Mg doping, the Fermi level is reduced and it requires lesser energy to excite from the valence to conduction bands. This is causing shifting of absorption edge to the lower energy and higher wavelength. This decreased bandgap is also favorable in collecting and transporting the photoelectrons. However, after 3% doping, there is a chance of crystal deformation so 3 wt % Mg was considered optimal for photocurrent emergence. The increase in absorption is also affected by porosity and mesoporous structure and higher oxygen vacancies which are essential in obtaining high efficiency. Typically, the pendent oxygen groups on the surface produce surface states, with lower energy [43]. In such cases, oxygen vacancies play the role of donor electrons.

Thermal stability is an important aspect to detect the thermal stability and validity of materials measured by Thermogravitational analysis (T.G.A). As PSCs are processed at a low temperature so thermal stability is not of much concern; however, we checked the thermal stability of Mg-doped ZnO as compared to undoped ZnO to confirm the thermal response of the samples. As thermal stability is mostly for nanopowders, so sol was dried and converted into powder form. TG/DTA curves of undoped ZnO and Mg-doped Zinc oxide powders followed by calcination are reported in Fig. 5 (a and b). Mainly weight loss occurs in the range of 150–800 °C. In this range, a weight loss of 30% for undoped zinc oxide powder and 27% for Mg-doped zinc oxide powder is being detected. In this range, all the organic, hydroxyl, and isopropoxide ligands are removed in Zinc oxide. Undoped ZnO powder has more than 30% weight loss as compared to doped because of less thermal stability. With the incorporation of Mg into ZnO lattice, it becomes highly resistant against heat and changes into a highly crystalline structure with strong cohesive forces present between these molecules [44]. Weight loss tends to seize after 800 °C.

From Differential thermal analysis (D.T.A) curves, there is only a single sharp exothermic reaction peak in both graphs. In the case of undoped zinc oxide lattice, the exothermic peak is formed at a range of 225 °C, however, doped with Mg causes an earlier exothermic peak appearing at 210 °C. this exothermic reaction is related to phase change reaction, which starts earlier in doped nanofibers. Undoped zinc oxide requires more heat of crystallization and its phase transition occurs at more temperature as compared to doped. It is evident from DT curves that the doped zinc oxide sample shows crystallization at a lower temperature than undoped zinc oxide [45]. In doped ZnO, Mg causes strain hardening as well as linked chain crosslinking due to cohesive forces, resulting in crystallization at lower temperatures.

DC-Hall effect system was used to measure the conductivity, sheet resistance, and sheet carrier mobility. Conductivity is related to the conduction of electrons by enhancing sheet carrier mobility, hence both are directly related. Resistance or sheet resistance are the hindrances in the path of electrons, thereby reducing the PSCs'efficiencies. Electron transport materials should exhibit higher conductivity and sheet carrier mobility. All the analyzed parameters are listed in Table 2 . As we increase the dopant concentration, resistivity is decreased, thereby increasing conductivity. 3 wt% Mg-ZnO show the lowest resistivity as 6.61 × 103 Ohm-cm, and the highest conductivity 1.59 × 10-4 1/Ohm-cm. Also, the maximum value of sheet carrier mobility (μ) 3.21 × 10-2cm2/Vs was obtained by 3 wt% Mg-ZnO, and the sheet resistance 3.34 × 108 Ohm-sq. The pure Zinc oxide sample gives a high resistivity which is 9.28 × 103 Ω cm. The decrease in resistivity for 3% Mg-ZnO can be attributed due to the incorporation of Mg2+ which supports the conduction of electrons mobility. Also, when Mg2+ is doped, the diameter of fibers reduces, which directly increases the conduction of electrons because now there is an aligned path for electrons transportation in the target directions [46]. Sheet carrier mobility is also increased in the same manner because when we replace Mg2+ into ZnO because it introduces an increase in the concentration of electrons in the doped samples. Additionally, a sharp decrease in the sheet resistance values was obtained, suggesting that the decrease in diameter caused such results. However, if we further add Mg concentration, the properties are reversed. Because, at higher than the optimal concentration of doping, the structure is deformed and there is an increase in the grain scattering [47].

The performance of the fabricated PSCs was evaluated by the current density (J)-voltage (V) measurements of the cell. J-V curves of the fabricated PSCs at pure ZnO and 1, 2, 3 wt % Mg-doped ZnO are shown in Fig. 6 (a) . The efficiency of solar cells was computed by examining open-circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF), and current density. These performance variables are linked to each other via the following relation (Equation (6)):
(Equation 6)
η
=
Voc . Jsc . FF
Pin

In this equation, η represents the efficiency of the fabricated solar cell, Voc is for open-circuit voltage, the current density is represented by Jsc and FF is the useful energy extractable from the curve [48,49]. All these values of the samples are also listed in Table 3 and Fig. 6(a). It is clear from the Figure that with the increasing wt % Mg into ZnO, the current density is increased as incase of pure ZnO, Jsc value is 11.80 mA but with the addition of Mg dopant, Jsc is increased as 17.75 mA, 20.67, and 25.06 mA. With the increase of dopant concentration, Jsc is significantly improved, which can be due to better band alignment [50,51]. Suitable band alignment favors the flow of electrons from the conduction band of perovskite to the conduction band of ZnO [52,53]. Also, Mg doping was effective in continuous electronic pathways, hence better electrical conductivity results in improved FF. The combined effect of band alignment and conduction suppresses the electron-hole recombinations and their energy barrier is maintained, hence an effective charge separation can be achieved [44].

Mg has a vital role to establish the fill factor by facilitating the electrons' transportation paths, conductivity, and hence efficiency of ZnO electron transport layer based PSCs [54]. For pure ZnO, PSCs efficiency is 6.09%, however, for 1, 2, and 3 wt% Mg-doped ZnO PSCs it is 8.48, 10.33, and 13.52% for Mg-doped ZnO. Tuning of bandgap results in better diffusion of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, which results in open circuit values are also enhanced [55]. Due to this, the efficiency is increased up to 13.52%. Interfacial engineering by surface modification of ETL layer via Mg doping of perovskite devices results in higher efficiency and tuned fermi level. Also, it supports more and more electrons transfer, fewer recombinations over the ZnO nanofibers ETM. The performance of our proposed Mg-doped ZnO nano-fibers was comparable to that of previously reported nano-fibers with other metal dopants as shown in Table 4 . Compared to other metals, low cost and earth abundant nature of Mg provides an advantage in commercial application.

Photoactive textiles can significantly controle the disease spread, hence their demand during the COVID outbreak has drastically increased. To apply the ZnO as an antibacterial agent, we dispersed calcined ZnO nano-fibers (3 wt%) in the 2% solution of acrylate binder. The nano-dispersion of ZnO nano-fibers was applied on cotton fabric via dip-dry-cure method. Antibacterial activity of fabrics treated with undoped and Mg-doped ZnO with respect was investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 (b). The observation revealed that antibacterial activity of Mg doped ZnO is better than undoped ZnO. In undoped ZnO, smaller inhibition zone is formed around the treated fabric sample, hence it shows less antibacterial activity. In Mg doped ZnO, zone of inhibition of bacterial growth is much higher. This better antibacterial effect is from the photocatalytic effect of ZnO nano-fibers, where exciton (electron hole pair) is produced as result of photon absorption [56]. The redox reaction of exciton with ambient moisture generates ROS, which destroy bacterias. Stronger photocatalytic produces higher vicinity around the active material; hence growth of bacterias in that vicinity is restricted. Thus, Mg doped ZnO degrades the bacteria more actively and creates a larger inhibition zone around itself. Whereas, untreated cotton fabric has no inhibition zone, and growth of bacterial colonies can be observed on the fabric surface.

In summary, Mg-doped ZnO electrospun Nanofibers of different concentrations (1, 2, and 3 wt %) were synthesized by the electrospinning and investigated their suitability for photoanode materials. XRD results suggested that Mg-incorporated hexagonal zinc oxide has consistently formed and there is an increase in intensities of peaks after Mg doping. FTIR analysis showed that functional groups like hydroxyl groups are present which supports the photocatalytic activity of nanofibers. SEM images are revealing that all the nanofibers are uniform and nanoporous with thickness in nanometric ranges. U.V–vis spectroscopy has shown an increase in absorption by Mg addition and reduction of band gap simultaneously. PSCs fabricated using undoped, 1 wt % Mg-doped and 2 and 3 wt % Mg-doped ZnO were calculated to be 6.02, 8.48, 10.33, and 13.52%, respectively. PSC based on 3.0 wt % Mg-doped ZnO photoanode exhibits much higher photovoltaic performance among all the samples and also shows high conduction as well as carrier mobility. Moreover, Mg-doped ZnO showed better photoactivity against bacterial growth inhibition, when applied on the fabric.
